data_flow_trace.original: SYSTEM_CALLER calls NativeOracle.record(sourceType, sourceId, nonce=200, ...) at src/oracle/NativeOracle.sol:74
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: record() still gated by requireAllowed(SYSTEM_CALLER) and delegates nonce check to _updateNonce before invoking callback.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Same entry point; signature and flow unchanged.

data_flow_trace.original: _updateNonce allows any nonce > currentNonce, so nonce=200 overwrites _nonces while currentNonce=100
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: The check has been tightened to strict sequentiality: 'if (nonce != currentNonce + 1) revert Errors.NonceNotSequential(...)'. Only nonce == currentNonce+1 is accepted.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Original vulnerable check was 'if (nonce <= currentNonce) revert NonceNotIncreasing'. Now it is 'if (nonce != currentNonce + 1) revert NonceNotSequential'. The NonceNotIncreasing error no longer exists in Errors.sol — only NonceNotSequential remains.

data_flow_trace.original: Nonces 101-199 become permanently unreachable after jump to 200
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Jumping to 200 when current is 100 now reverts, so _nonces stays at 100 and 101..199 remain the only acceptable next values. No gap can form from SYSTEM_CALLER input.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Attack precondition (ability to skip nonces) is removed.

data_flow_trace.original: Bridge payload at nonce=150 never reaches _invokeCallback, GBridgeReceiver.onOracleEvent never fires
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Because the skip is impossible, every in-sequence nonce reaches _invokeCallback. GBridgeReceiver._handlePortalMessage processes in order; comments at GBridgeReceiver.sol:30-32,84-85 explicitly state that replay protection now relies on NativeOracle's sequential enforcement.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Requires the previous step to succeed, which it cannot.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: GBridgeReceiver refactored; the old _processedNonces map is deprecated (renamed __deprecated_processedNonces at GBridgeReceiver.sol:30-32).

data_flow_trace.original: _processedNonces[messageNonce] stays false; mint precompile never executes
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: The _processedNonces map referenced by the report no longer exists as active state (deprecated slot preserved only for storage layout). The mint-precompile call still happens in _handlePortalMessage, and it will fire for every in-order nonce.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Any in-order oracle record hitting the bridge callback mints.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Replay protection model was redesigned. Even if a skip were possible, the exploit description's mechanism (stale _processedNonces) no longer applies.

data_flow_trace.original: isSyncedPast(..., 150) returns true (latestNonce=200>=150), false-positive masking dropped event
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: isSyncedPast still uses 'latestNonce > 0 && latestNonce >= nonce'. The semantic would still mis-report if a gap existed, but a gap cannot occur due to the sequential enforcement at step 2.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Needs a preceding nonce skip, which is impossible.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: View logic unchanged, but the preimage (skippable nonces) is eliminated.

data_flow_trace.original: getRecord(..., 150) returns zero-struct; user's 100 ETH locked with no mint
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: getRecord still returns a default DataRecord for unknown nonces, but this only matters if a gap exists. No gap is possible under the current _updateNonce.

reasoning_chain: The report assumes _updateNonce allows any nonce strictly greater than currentNonce, which is the pattern in the quoted snippet ('if (nonce <= currentNonce) revert NonceNotIncreasing'). At commit a623eab the function has been rewritten at src/oracle/NativeOracle.sol:257-270 to enforce strict sequentiality: 'if (nonce != currentNonce + 1) revert Errors.NonceNotSequential(sourceType, sourceId, currentNonce + 1, nonce);'. The Errors.sol file at :412 now only defines NonceNotSequential (NonceNotIncreasing has been removed). Tests at test/unit/oracle/NativeOracle.t.sol:136-158 explicitly verify that a jump from nonce=1 to nonce=5 reverts with NonceNotSequential — exactly the exploit precondition. GBridgeReceiver has also been refactored (src/oracle/evm/native_token_bridge/GBridgeReceiver.sol:30-32,84-85): the _processedNonces mapping is deprecated and replay protection is delegated to the oracle's sequential enforcement. Because SYSTEM_CALLER can no longer skip nonces, nonce=150 can never be silently dropped; any attempt to submit a later event first reverts until 150 is recorded. The exploit's core data-flow step (writing _nonces=200 while leaving 150 unpopulated) is infeasible.

key_evidence.why_matters: This is the exact check the report claims is missing. The current implementation enforces strict +1 sequentiality, making any nonce skip impossible at the entry point.

key_evidence.excerpt: error NonceNotSequential(uint32 sourceType, uint256 sourceId, uint128 expectedNonce, uint128 providedNonce);
key_evidence.why_matters: Confirms the legacy NonceNotIncreasing error (referenced verbatim in the report) has been replaced with a stricter sequentiality error.
Documents that the design explicitly relies on sequential oracle nonces — skips are assumed impossible by construction.
A direct regression test asserting that jumping from nonce=1 to nonce=5 reverts — the precise exploit gadget the report describes.

counterargument: One could argue the threat is not fully extinguished: if the off-chain consensus engine (SYSTEM_CALLER) genuinely loses the payload for nonce=150 (e.g., validator bug drops it before submission), then the chain is now stuck — every subsequent record reverts until 150 is provided, creating a liveness/DoS on the bridge rather than a silent fund loss. This is a strictly different (and arguably worse for liveness) risk profile, and it is a deliberate design trade-off acknowledged by GBridgeReceiver's comments. It does not restore the vulnerability claimed in the report, which was about a silent skip + false isSyncedPast — that path is now impossible. An additional counter is that a malicious/compromised SYSTEM_CALLER could still refuse to submit nonce=150 indefinitely, locking all later bridge events, but this is a trust-root failure (SYSTEM_CALLER is the consensus engine) rather than an oracle contract bug, and it cannot produce the specific data-flow outcome (isSyncedPast=true while getRecord=0) that the report describes.
